# Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

February 11, 2025 issue

104 ( 3 ) e210286

<https://doi.org/10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286>

Letters to the Editor

Track Citations Add to favorites

[](https://s100.copyright.com/AppDispatchServlet?publisherName=WoltersKluwer&publication=wnl&title=Long-Term+Intake+of+Red+Meat+in+Relation+to+Dementia+Risk+and+Cognitive+Function+in+US+Adults&publicationDate=2025-01-15&author=Yuhan+Li%2C+Yanping+Li%2C+Xiao+Gu%2C+Yuxi+Liu%2C+et+al.&contentID=10.1212%2FWNL.0000000000210286&volumeNum=104&issueNum=3&numPages=13&orderBeanReset=true)

Get Access

## Abstract

### Background and Objectives

Previous studies have shown inconsistent associations between red meat intake and cognitive health. Our objective was to examine the association between red meat intake and multiple cognitive outcomes.

### Methods

In this prospective cohort study, we included participants free of dementia at baseline from 2 nationwide cohort studies in the United States: the Nurses' Health Study (NHS) and the Health Professionals Follow-Up Study (HPFS). Diets were assessed using a validated semiquantitative food frequency questionnaire. We ascertained incident dementia cases from both NHS participants (1980–2023) and HPFS participants (1986–2023). Objective cognitive function was assessed using the Telephone Interview for Cognitive Status (1995–2008) among a subset of NHS participants. Subjective cognitive decline (SCD) was self-reported by NHS participants (2012, 2014) and HPFS participants (2012, 2016). Cox proportional hazards models, general linear regression, and Poisson regression models were applied to assess the associations between red meat intake and different cognitive outcomes.

### Results

The dementia analysis included 133,771 participants (65.4% female) with a mean baseline age of 48.9 years, the objective cognitive function analysis included 17,458 female participants with a mean baseline age of 74.3 years, and SCD analysis included 43,966 participants (77.1% female) with a mean baseline age of 77.9 years. Participants with processed red meat intake ≥0.25 serving per day, compared with <0.10 serving per day, had a 13% higher risk of dementia (hazard ratio [HR] 1.13; 95% CI 1.08–1.19; _p_ <sub>linearity</sub> < 0.001) and a 14% higher risk of SCD (relative risk [RR] 1.14; 95% CI 1.04–1.25; _p_ <sub>linearity</sub> = 0.004). Higher processed red meat intake was associated with accelerated aging in global cognition (1.61 years per 1 serving per day increment [95% CI 0.20–3.03]) and in verbal memory (1.69 years per 1 serving per day increment [95% CI 0.13–3.25], both _p_ <sub>linearity</sub> = 0.03). Unprocessed red meat intake of ≥1.00 serving per day, compared with <0.50 serving per day, was associated with a 16% higher risk of SCD (RR 1.16; 95% CI 1.03–1.30; _p_ <sub>linearity</sub> = 0.04). Replacing 1 serving per day of nuts and legumes for processed red meat was associated with a 19% lower risk of dementia (HR 0.81, 95% CI 0.75–0.86), 1.37 fewer years of cognitive aging (95% CI −2.49 to −0.25), and a 21% lower risk of SCD (RR 0.79, 95% CI 0.68–0.92).

### Discussion

Higher intake of red meat, particularly processed red meat, was associated with a higher risk of developing dementia and worse cognition. Reducing red meat consumption could be included in dietary guidelines to promote cognitive health. Further research is needed to assess the generalizability of these findings to populations with diverse ethnic backgrounds.

Letters to the Editor

14 August 2025

Author Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Yuhan Li| Department of Epidemiology; Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine; Department of Nutrition | Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA; Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA, Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA; Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, Cambridge, MA

Dong D. Wang| Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine; | Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA;

We sincerely thank Dr. Montoya and colleagues for their comments on our study on long-term red meat intake and cognitive outcomes. <sup>1</sup>

Regarding the authors’ comments on the use of semi-quantitative food frequency questionnaires (FFQs), we would like to clarify that our FFQs have been extensively validated and have shown validity and reproducibility for measuring food and nutrient intakes, <sup>2</sup> including red meat intake. While all dietary assessment tools are subject to some degree of measurement error, the errors in FFQ-derived intake are largely random and would thus attenuate true associations, leading to conservative (i.e., underestimated) effect estimates. To further reduce random error and better capture long-term habitual intake, we calculated cumulative average intake using repeated dietary assessments collected every 2–4 years over more than 4 decades. Additionally, our sensitivity analyses yielded consistent associations, reinforcing the robustness of our findings.

We agree that the observational study design cannot completely rule out the possibility of residual confounding. However, we adjusted for a comprehensive set of covariates, many of which were updated over time using repeated measurements. Moreover, because all participants are health professionals, we were able to collect highly detailed and accurate data, and the findings are less likely to be confounded by socioeconomic status or access to healthcare. These design features help to minimize residual confounding. In a prior publication based on these cohorts, we have also demonstrated that residual confounding was unlikely to account for even modest observed associations between dietary factors and health outcomes. <sup>3</sup>

As an observational study, our objective was to examine associations rather than establish causality, and we have not drawn causal conclusions. While the observed hazard ratio of 1.13 may appear modest, it carries important public health implications given the widespread and relatively high consumption of processed red meat in the US population and the growing number of dementia cases nationally. Even small relative risks can translate into a substantial population-attributable risk, <sup>4,5</sup> suggesting that a meaningful number of dementia cases could potentially be prevented with reduced intake of processed red meat. Moreover, our substitution analysis, which showed a 19% lower risk of dementia when one daily serving of processed meat was replaced with nuts and legumes, highlights actionable dietary recommendations for cognitive health.

#### References:

1. Li Y, Li Y, Gu X, et al. Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286
2. Gu X, Wang DD, Sampson L, et al. Validity and Reproducibility of a Semiquantitative Food Frequency Questionnaire for Measuring Intakes of Foods and Food Groups. _Am J Epidemiol_ . 2024;193(1):170-179. doi:10.1093/aje/kwad170
3. Bao Y, Han J, Hu FB, et al. Association of nut consumption with total and cause-specific mortality. _N Engl J Med_ . 2013;369(21):2001-2011. doi:10.1056/NEJMoa1307352
4. Manson JE, Colditz GA, Stampfer MJ, et al. A prospective study of obesity and risk of coronary heart disease in women. _N Engl J Med_ . 1990;322(13):882-889. doi:10.1056/NEJM199003293221303
5. Rothman KJ, Lash TL, VanderWeele TJ, Haneuse S. _Modern Epidemiology_ . 4th ed. Lippincott Williams & Wilkins; 2020.

Author Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Yuhan Li| Department of Epidemiology; Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine | Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA; Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA

Dong D. Wang| Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine; Department of Nutrition | Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School, Boston, MA; Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health, Boston, MA; Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard, Cambridge, MA

We sincerely thank Dr. Brenner for highlighting the potential biological mechanism of N-glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc) as a potential explanation of the associations between red meat intake and cognitive health outcomes identified by our study. <sup>1</sup>

Our study proposed several mechanistic pathways to explain associations between intake of unprocessed and processed red meat and dementia risk and cognitive function. Dr. Brenner’s suggestion regarding Neu5Gc, a dietary-derived xenoantigen commonly found in red meat, provides a valuable perspective that enhances our current understanding of these associations.

Specifically, interactions between Neu5Gc and circulating anti-Neu5Gc antibodies may trigger inflammatory responses, potentially contributing to an increased risk of cognitive decline. <sup>2,3</sup> Such mechanisms could offer a biologically plausible explanation for the observed associations between red meat intake and cognitive health. We agree with Dr. Brenner that further investigation into this pathway could yield significant insights, potentially informing dietary recommendations for dementia prevention.

#### References:

1. Li Y, Li Y, Gu X, et al. Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi: 10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286
2. Samraj AN, Pearce OMT, Läubli H, et al. A red meat–derived glycan promotes inflammation and cancer progression. _Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A_ . 2015;112(2):542-547. doi: 10.1073/pnas.1417508112

9 May 2025

Author Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Yuhan Li| Department of Epidemiology; Department of Medicine | Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health; Channing Division of Network Medicine, Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School

Dong D. Wang| Department of Medicine; Department of Nutrition; Broad Institute | Channing Division of Network Medicine, Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School; Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health; MIT and Harvard

We sincerely thank Dr. Gagneux and colleagues for their comments on our paper, <sup>1</sup> and on the role of xenosialitis and diet-derived non-human sialic acid N-glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc) in the biological mechanism of cognitive decline associated with red meat intake. While our paper discussed several mechanisms broadly, we agree that further investigation into the proinflammatory effects of Neu5Gc is needed to comprehensively understand the biological pathways underlying the development of cognitive impairment.

Xeno-autoantigen formation, combined with its interaction with circulating anti-Neu5Gc antibodies and the subsequent inflammation, offers a framework that connects dietary intakes, inflammatory processes, and cognitive health. <sup>2,3</sup> Further exploration of these interactions in the context of cognitive impairment may yield novel insights into the diet-related pathophysiological mechanisms of dementia.

We also agree with Gagneux et al. that future studies could focus on assessing the effects of dietary Neu5Gc content and the potential mitigating impact of N-acetylneuraminic acid supplementation on neurovascular inflammation in humans. Such research may refine current dietary recommendations and inform targeted nutritional interventions aimed at slowing cognitive decline.

Author Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Yuhan Li| Department of Epidemiology; Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine | Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health; Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School

Dong D. Wang| Channing Division of Network Medicine, Department of Medicine; Department of Nutrition; Broad Institute of MIT and Harvard | Brigham and Women's Hospital and Harvard Medical School; Harvard T. H. Chan School of Public Health

We sincerely thank Drs. Hirani and Etienne for their comments on our study on red meat intake and cognitive health outcomes. <sup>1</sup>

Regarding the suggestion to stratify participants based on vegetarian/vegan status, we agree that examining vegetarian/vegan status and adherence to plant-based dietary patterns could offer additional valuable insights. The inclusion of plant-based dietary patterns in future studies is indeed of great interest to us, especially as we expand our research on dietary factors, dementia, and cognitive function.

We also appreciate the readers’ comments on our proposed pathway involving host-microbe co-metabolism and the role of trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO), a metabolite derived from L-carnitine in red meat, which has been implicated in cognitive aging. <sup>2,3</sup> This area is an exciting direction for further research, and we hope to explore this mediator in greater depth in future studies to provide a comprehensive understanding of red meat’s role in neurodegenerative processes.

Last, we agree with the readers’ point about the limitations on the generalizability of our findings due to our predominantly White health care professional cohorts. As noted in our paper’s Discussion section, including more diverse populations in future studies will be crucial for further confirming our findings.

8 April 2025

Reader Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Steven R. Brenner | Neurology Department, retired | St. Louis University

I read the article by Li with interest, with respect to red meat and processed meat increasing the risk of dementia. <sup>1</sup>

Red meat contains N-glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc), which is a xenoantigen incorporated into human glycans through metabolism, and is associated with cardiovascular disease and inflammation, which are likely to cause breakdown of the blood-brain barrier.

Pig organs contain high levels of neu5Gc and the concentration increases with cooking, <sup>2,3</sup> which could occur with cooking while processing meat. Neu5Gc is incorporated from the diet through metabolism into the vascular endothelium. <sup>4</sup> The endothelium is important in maintaining a functioning blood-brain barrier, with impairment leading to neurodegeneration. <sup>5</sup>

Breakdown of the blood-brain barrier through inflammation from the xenoantigen neu5Gc may be a contributing factor to dementia from consumption of red meat and processed meat.

1. Li Y, Li Y, Gu X, et al. Long-term intake of red meat in relation to dementia risk and cognitive function in US adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi: 10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286

28 July 2025

Reader Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Mateo Amaya Montoya| Internal Medicine Department | Fundación Santa Fe de Bogotá

Carmen Suescun Zuleta| Internal Medicine Department | Fundación Santa Fe de Bogotá

Pedro Ivan Navarro Naranjo| Ophthalmology Section, Cornea and External Diseases | Asociación Médica de los Andes

Fernando Sierra Arango| Gastroenterology and Hepatology Section, Internal Medicine Department | Fundación Santa Fe de Bogotá

We read with great interest the article by Li et al., <sup>1</sup> which reports that long-term intake of processed red meat is associated with greater dementia risk and cognitive decline. However, several methodological limitations may have weakened this conclusion, particularly the reliance on self-reported, semiquantitative food frequency questionnaires. These are prone to recall bias, social desirability bias, and exposure misclassification.

One concern is the potential for healthy user bias. Participants with lower processed meat intake also reported more health-promoting behaviors such as greater physical activity, moderate alcohol use, and lower smoking prevalence. Comorbidities like hypertension and diabetes were also less prevalent. Although these factors were included in adjusted models, their self-reported nature and the possibility of residual misclassification suggest that processed meat may function more as a lifestyle proxy than as an independent risk factor.

Moreover, the observed association is extremely modest. The reported hazard ratio of 1.13 for dementia translates to a Cohen’s d of approximately 0.067—well below the threshold for even a small effect. Such a subtle association, while statistically significant, remains highly vulnerable to residual confounding, selection bias, and exposure misclassification, and thus should be interpreted with appropriate methodological caution when considering causal implications.

#### Reference(s):

1. Li Y, Li Y, Gu X, et al. Long-term intake of red meat in relation to dementia risk and cognitive function in US adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi:10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286

13 February 2025

Reader Response: Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults

Rahim Hirani | School of medicine, New York Medical College

Mill Etienne| Department of Neurology | New York Medical College

We read with great interest the recent article by Li et al.; we commend the authors for their analysis of the relationship between red meat consumption and cognitive health outcomes. <sup>1</sup> This study provides valuable insights into dietary impacts on cognition. While comparing lower and higher red meat intake is crucial, stratifying participants based on vegetarian/vegan status could enhance the interpretability of findings. Although the regression analysis included some dietary patterns, data on individuals who do not consume meat remain absent. Expanding future cohorts to capture these populations may reveal unique cognitive health benefits associated with plant-based diets. <sup>2</sup> However, we recognize the challenges of such stratification in a decades-long longitudinal study.

Additionally, we were particularly intrigued by the study’s mention of  trimethylamine N-oxide (TMAO) as a gut microbiome marker. Future research could further investigate how red meat influences gut microbiota composition and its downstream effects on brain health. Exploring the microbiome as a mediator could offer a nuanced understanding of red meat’s role in cognition.

Lastly, we note the study’s limited racial and ethnic diversity; participants were predominantly White. We encourage future work to examine whether these findings generalize to other populations and to consider the potential intersectionality of race, ethnicity, and sex in dietary impacts on cognition. By addressing these areas, future studies could further refine dietary recommendations for promoting cognitive health.

#### References:

1. Li Y, Li Y, Gu X, et al. Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi: 10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286
2. Liu X, Dhana K, Barnes LL, et al. A healthy plant-based diet was associated with slower cognitive decline in African American older adults: a biracial community-based cohort. _Am J Clin Nutr_ . 2022;116(4):875-886. doi: 10.1093/ajcn/nqac204

5 May 2025

Reader Response: Long-term intake of red meat in relation to dementia risk and cognitive function in US adults

Juston W. Jaco| Departments of Pathology and Anthropology | Center for Academic Research and Training in Anthropogeny (CARTA)

Pascal Gagneux | Biomedical Sciences, Center for Academic Research and Training in Anthropogeny (CARTA)

Nissi Varki | Pathology, Center for Academic Research and Training in Anthropogeny (CARTA)

Ajit Varki | Cellular and Molecular Medicine , Center for Academic Research and Training in Anthropogeny (CARTA)

We read with interest the recent study by Li et al. <sup>1</sup> and the related _BMJ_ commentary by Dyer <sup>2</sup> linking red meat consumption to cognitive decline. While the authors discuss several potential mechanisms, they do not mention xenosialitis—a chronic inflammatory response triggered by dietary incorporation of the nonhuman sialic acid _N-_ glycolylneuraminic acid (Neu5Gc) enriched in red meat into human cells. <sup>3</sup> This metabolic incorporation generates xeno-autoantigens that can interact with circulating anti-Neu5Gc antibodies, promoting vascular inflammation, <sup>4</sup> a key factor in dementia.

Neu5Gc-rich diets combined with anti-Neu5Gc antibodies elevate systemic inflammatory markers in _Cmah_ \-null mice, a sialic acid-humanized model. <sup>3</sup> Endothelial Neu5Gc accumulation enhances complement activation, cytokine release, and selectin expression. <sup>5</sup> Thus, xeno-autoantigen/xeno-autoantibody interactions, already implicated in cancer risk, may also accelerate cerebrovascular disease <sup>4</sup> impairing cerebral blood flow and contributing to cognitive decline.

In addition to reducing red meat intake, dietary strategies could focus on the Neu5Gc content of foods and modulating Neu5Gc incorporation. For instance, increasing dietary _N_ \-acetylneuraminic acid, the precursor to Neu5Gc (enriched in poultry and some dairy products), can competitively inhibit Neu5Gc incorporation, thus mitigating xenosialitis. <sup>4</sup> Future studies should explore the role of dietary Neu5Gc and anti-Neu5Gc antibodies in neurovascular inflammation in humans to refine recommendations for cognitive health.

References:

1. Li Y, Gu X, Liu Y, et al. Long-Term Intake of Red Meat in Relation to Dementia Risk and Cognitive Function in US Adults. _Neurology_ . 2025;104(3):e210286. doi: 10.1212/WNL.0000000000210286
2. Dyer O. Large US study links processed red meat to dementia risk. _BMJ_ . 2025; 388:r126. doi: 10.1136/bmj.r126
3. Samraj AN, Pearce OM, Läubli H et al. A red meat-derived glycan promotes inflammation and cancer progression. _Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A_ . 2015;112(2):542-547. doi: 10.1073/pnas.1417508112
4. Kawanishi K, Coker JK, Grunddal KV et al. Dietary Neu5Ac Intervention Protects Against Atherosclerosis Associated With Human-Like Neu5Gc Loss-Brief Report. _Arterioscler Thromb Vasc Biol_ . 2021;41(11):2730-2739. doi: 10.1161/ATVBAHA.120.315280
5. Hedlund M, Padler-Karavani V, Varki NM, Varki A. Evidence for a human-specific mechanism for diet and antibody-mediated inflammation in carcinoma progression. _Proc Natl Acad Sci U S A_ . 2008;105(48):18936-18941. doi: 10.1073/pnas.0803943105
